chunk_summary: This section provides a consolidated classification system for undernutrition in children under five, focusing on anthropometry, oedema, and risk-based care decisions for severe and moderate acute malnutrition (SAM/MAM). It emphasizes clinical assessment, appetite testing, and appropriate inpatient or outpatient care pathways.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for classifying undernutrition in children under five, including WHZ/WLZ, MUAC, and oedema.
Differentiate between complicated and uncomplicated SAM and determine appropriate care settings.
Learn the five decision-oriented outputs for undernutrition classification and their clinical implications.
Recognize the importance of early risk containment in cases of complicated SAM.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined as WHZ/WLZ < -3 SD and/or MUAC <115 mm in children aged 6–59 months.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined as WHZ/WLZ ≥ -3 to < -2 SD and/or MUAC 115 to <125 mm in children aged 6–59 months, with no oedema.
Nutritional oedema (bilateral pitting oedema) is treated as at least SAM, with severe oedema or danger signs requiring inpatient care.
Stunting is defined as HAZ < -2 SD, while underweight is defined as WAZ < -2 SD with normal WHZ/WLZ and HAZ.
Complicated SAM requires immediate risk containment, including prevention of hypoglycemia and hypothermia, and avoiding large-volume fluids unless strict shock criteria are met.
reference_quote: "A practical classification should unify anthropometry (WHZ/WLZ, MUAC), oedema, and the split that predicts risk and site of care most reliably: complicated versus uncomplicated SAM."
document_key_topics: Classification of undernutrition in children under five
Criteria for SAM and MAM diagnosis
